product.one_line_description: The GitHub REST API allows users to create integrations, retrieve data, and automate workflows.

authentication.auth_summary: Authentication to the GitHub REST API can be performed using personal access tokens (fine-grained or classic), tokens generated by GitHub Apps or OAuth Apps, or the built-in GITHUB_TOKEN in GitHub Actions workflows. These tokens are typically sent in the 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR-TOKEN' or 'Authorization: token YOUR-TOKEN' header. For some GitHub App and OAuth App endpoints, basic authentication using the app's client ID and client secret is required. Username and password authentication is not supported.

developer_access.access_notes: Developers can obtain credentials without human approval. Personal access tokens can be created by users for personal use. GitHub App tokens can be generated through the app's settings page, where client IDs and secrets can be found and generated by the app owner or manager. The GITHUB_TOKEN is a built-in token automatically available within GitHub Actions workflows.

api_surface.api_summary: The GitHub REST API is public, versioned, and fully described in an OpenAPI compliant document, enabling a broad range of functionalities including creating integrations, retrieving data, automating workflows, managing billing and budgets, and building CI servers. It supports authentication via various token types (PATs, GitHub App tokens, GITHUB_TOKEN) and offers extensive documentation with 199 related articles, indicating a comprehensive API surface. Examples show usage with `curl` for REST calls and `gh api` for CLI access.

mcp.mcp_summary: No information regarding Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers was found in the provided documentation.

buildability.verdict_reasoning: Self-serve developer access is verified with a broad/moderate documented public API.

evidence_pool.claim: The GitHub REST API allows users to create integrations, retrieve data, and automate workflows.
evidence_pool.source_title: GitHub REST API documentation - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: Create integrations, retrieve data, and automate your workflows with the GitHub REST API.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet:
After creating a token, you can authenticate your request by sending the token in the `Authorization` header of your request. For example, in the following request, replace `YOUR-TOKEN` with a reference to your token:
```shell
curl --request GET \
--url "https://api.github.com/octocat" \
--header "Authorization: Bearer YOUR-TOKEN" \
--header "X-GitHub-Api-Version: 2026-03-10"
```

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: In most cases, you can use `Authorization: Bearer` or `Authorization: token` to pass a token. However, if you are passing a JSON web token (JWT), you must use `Authorization: Bearer`.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: Some REST API endpoints for GitHub Apps and OAuth apps require you to use basic authentication to access the endpoint. You will use the app's client ID as the username and the app's client secret as the password.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: You can also create an OAuth token with an OAuth app to access the REST API. However, GitHub recommends that you use a GitHub App instead.

evidence_pool.claim: Authentication to the GitHub REST API can be performed using personal access tokens (fine-grained or classic), tokens generated by GitHub Apps or OAuth Apps, or the built-in GITHUB_TOKEN in GitHub Actions workflows. These tokens are typically sent in the 'Authorization: Bearer YOUR-TOKEN' or 'Authorization: token YOUR-TOKEN' header. For some GitHub App and OAuth App endpoints, basic authentication using the app's client ID and client secret is required. Username and password authentication is not supported.
evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: To authenticate your request, you will need to provide an authentication token with the required scopes or permissions. There a few different ways to get a token: You can create a personal access token, generate a token with a GitHub App, or use the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` in a GitHub Actions workflow.

For some GitHub App and OAuth App endpoints, basic authentication using the app's client ID and client secret is required. Username and password authentication is not supported.

evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: Some REST API endpoints for GitHub Apps and OAuth apps require you to use basic authentication to access the endpoint. You will use the app's client ID as the username and the app's client secret as the password.

evidence_pool.claim: Authentication to the GitHub REST API can be performed using personal access tokens (fine-grained or classic), tokens generated by GitHub Apps or OAuth Apps, or the built-in GITHUB_TOKEN in GitHub Actions workflows. evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: Authentication with username and password is not supported. If you try to authenticate with user name and password, you will receive a 4xx error.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: If you want to use the GitHub REST API for personal use, you can create a personal access token.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: If you want to use the API for an organization or on behalf of another user, GitHub recommends that you use a GitHub App.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: You can create a personal access token, generate a token with a GitHub App, or use the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` in a GitHub Actions workflow.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: Next to **Client secrets**, click **Generate a new client secret** to generate a client secret for your app.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet:
If you are the owner of a GitHub App or OAuth app, or if you are an app manager for a GitHub App, you can find the client ID and generate a client secret on the settings page for your app. To navigate to your app's settings page:
...
Next to **Client secrets**, click **Generate a new client secret** to generate a client secret for your app.

evidence_pool.claim: Developers can obtain credentials without human approval. Personal access tokens can be created by users for personal use. evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: If you want to use the API in a GitHub Actions workflow, GitHub recommends that you authenticate with the built-in `GITHUB_TOKEN` instead of creating a token.

evidence_pool.source_title: GitHub REST API documentation - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet:
# GitHub REST API documentation

Create integrations, retrieve data, and automate your workflows with the GitHub REST API.

evidence_pool.source_title: GitHub REST API documentation - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet:
The REST API is now versioned. For more information, see "[About API versioning](/rest/overview/api-versions)."

# GitHub REST API documentation

evidence_pool.source_title: GitHub REST API documentation - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet:
### About the OpenAPI description for the REST API

The GitHub REST API is fully described in an OpenAPI compliant document.

evidence_pool.source_title: Authenticating to the REST API - GitHub Docs
evidence_pool.evidence_snippet:
### [Authenticating in a GitHub Actions workflow using GitHub CLI](#authenticating-in-a-github-actions-workflow-using-github-cli)

To make an authenticated request to the API in a GitHub Actions workflow using GitHub CLI, you can store the value of `GITHUB_TOKEN` as an environment variable, and use the `run` keyword to execute the GitHub CLI `api` subcommand.

evidence_pool.evidence_snippet: Showing 1-9 of 199

critic_analysis.developer_access.access_notes: The claim for `developer_access.access_notes` is a composite string. One of the supporting evidence snippets (source_url: https://docs.github.com/en/rest/authentication/authenticating-to-the-rest-api, claim_field: developer_access.access_notes) has a `match_type` of `not_found`. Although the `evidence_snippet` itself contains relevant information about generating client secrets for GitHub Apps, the `not_found` match type indicates that the automation could not establish a direct, explicit match between that specific piece of the claim and the evidence. Therefore, this composite field is challenged for lacking explicit, direct support for all its parts.
